questions.question: What is the primary sign that a 'Cocktail Party' pitch has failed?

options.text: The listener asks 'How does that work?'
The listener punches you
The listener says 'Oh, that's nice' and changes the subject
The listener asks for your business card

questions.explanation: 'Oh, that's nice' is polite code for 'I have no idea what you said, or I don't care.' A good pitch triggers curiosity and a follow-up question.

questions.question: Which pitch duration is used when you are just meeting someone casually?

options.text: 30 Seconds
2 Minutes
5 Minutes
20 Minutes

questions.explanation: The 30-second pitch is for high-level intros. Don't hold someone hostage with a 5-minute monologue before you know if they are interested.

questions.question: How should you adapt your pitch for an Investor vs. a Customer?

options.text: You shouldn't; keep it exactly the same.
Tell the investor about the product features; tell the customer about the market size.
Tell the investor about the Market Opportunity; tell the customer about the Outcome/Solution.
Lie to the investor; tell the truth to the customer.

questions.explanation: Investors buy equity (future growth/market size). Customers buy solutions (immediate pain relief). Tailor the value proposition to what they are buying.

questions.question: What is a common mistake technical founders make in pitches?

options.text: They speak too slowly.
They focus too much on the technology stack (React, Python, AI) instead of the problem.
They wear suits.
They don't have a business card.

questions.explanation: No customer cares that your backend is in Rust. They care that it doesn't crash. Focusing on specs instead of outcomes is the #1 technical founder trap.

questions.question: Why is it important to 'Practice Out Loud'?

options.text: To annoy your neighbors
Because reading in your head is different than speaking; you need to build muscle memory.
So you can memorize it word-for-word like a robot
It isn't important; improvising is better.

questions.explanation: Phrases that look good on paper often twist your tongue when spoken. You must practice the physical act of speaking the pitch to make it sound natural.

questions.question: What is the correct structure of the Creator Mission Statement formula?

options.text: I am a [Title] who has [Years of Experience] in [Industry].
I help [Specific Audience] go from [Point A Pain] to [Point B Transformation] through [Your Unique Method].
I sell [Product Type] for [Price] to [Anyone Who Will Pay].
I am passionate about [Topic] and love helping people.

questions.explanation: The Creator Mission Statement must be specific (who you help), have emotional stakes (the pain and destination), and set a clear expectation (your method). Generic 'passion' statements don't convert.

questions.question: Write your 30-Second 'Cocktail Party' pitch. Follow the 'Hook -> Pivot -> Proof' structure.
questions.promptForAI: Evaluate the pitch. Does it sound like a human conversation? Is it clear what the person does? Does it include a specific proof point?
